attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at 16 weeks gestation with a monochorionic monoamniotic twin pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy supplements. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated thus far with normal anatomy scans.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. An ultrasound scan shows both twins with normal growth, normal amniotic fluid and no evidence of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most important risk to discuss with this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has a monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA) twin pregnancy, which carries significant risks due to the shared amniotic sac. Cord entanglement is the most important risk to discuss as it occurs in up to 70% of MCMA pregnancies and is associated with significant perinatal mortality. Due to the shared amniotic space, the umbilical cords can become entangled, leading to compromise of blood flow and potential fetal death.

Discordant fetal anomaly risk, while a consideration in twin pregnancies, is less relevant in monochorionic twins as they arise from a single fertilised egg and share identical genetic material. While post-zygotic mutations can occur, leading to differences between twins, this is rare compared to the immediate risk of cord entanglement.

Discordant growth restriction, while possible in any twin pregnancy, is not the most critical risk in MCMA twins. The immediate threat of cord entanglement poses a more significant risk to fetal survival.

Conjoined twinning would have been identified on earlier ultrasound scans. While it is a possible complication of monochorionic monoamniotic twinning, it occurs very early in embryonic development and would be evident on the anatomy scan.

Vanishing twin syndrome typically occurs in the first trimester and is less relevant at 16 weeks gestation. Additionally, the ultrasound shows both twins with normal growth.

Due to the high risk of cord entanglement, MCMA twins require intensive surveillance from 24 weeks, typically including inpatient monitoring and regular ultrasound assessment. Delivery is usually recommended between 32-34 weeks gestation by caesarean section to prevent cord accidents.
learningPoints: Monochorionic monoamniotic twins have a high risk of cord entanglement and require intensive fetal surveillance from 24 weeks gestation.
Delivery by caesarean section at 32-34 weeks is recommended for monochorionic monoamniotic twins due to the risk of cord accidents.
Weekly ultrasound surveillance should assess cord entanglement, amniotic fluid, and fetal growth in monochorionic pregnancies.
references: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Twin and triplet pregnancy [Internet]. London: NICE; 2019 [cited 2024 Nov 17]. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng137/resources/twin-and-triplet-pregnancy-pdf-66141724389829

Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Management of Monochorionic Twin Pregnancy [Internet]. East Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2021 [cited 2024 Nov 17]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Management-Monochorionic-Twin-Pregnancy.pdf
